title: Fondue savoyarde
subtitle: Savoyard cheese fondue with Comté, Gruyère, white wine, and kirsch
excerpt: A pot of melted Comté and Gruyère loosened with dry white wine and a splash of kirsch, kept warm at the table for dipping cubes of bread. Fondue savoyarde is communal food from the French Alps, and it is one of the most forgiving things you can make for a crowd on a winter evening.
sourceNotes:
Philéas Gilbert and Emile Fetu, Le Livre de Cuisine (1900). Fondue preparation methods and cheese selection for the Savoyard style. Public domain.

A. Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903). The use of kirsch in fondue and the cornflour method for stabilising the emulsion. Public domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Grate the cheese and mix it with the cornflour up to a day ahead, then refrigerate. The wine can be measured out in advance. Make the fondue just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hot cheese dip from the Savoie and Haute-Savoie regions of the French Alps, made by melting local cheeses in dry white wine with a splash of kirsch. It is served at the table in a ceramic or cast-iron pot and eaten by dipping cubes of bread on long forks.

A clear cherry brandy distilled from fermented whole cherries, including the stones. It has a dry, faintly almond note from the stones and is used in fondue both for flavour and to help prevent the cheese from becoming stringy.

content.text: Fondue savoyarde is a pot of molten cheese kept warm at the table while everyone dips bread into it.
is made with Comté and Gruyère melted into dry white wine with a small amount of cornflour for stability and a splash of
at the end. The result is smooth and thick, with a nutty, slightly grassy flavour that no processed cheese product comes close to matching.

The two things that go wrong with fondue are: it splits into an oily puddle with a clump of stringy cheese, and it seizes into a solid mass. Both can be fixed. The cornflour keeps the proteins in the cheese from tightening, and the acid in the wine keeps the fats from separating. Add the cheese in small handfuls and stir in figures-of-eight rather than circles to keep the emulsion together. Too much heat at the wrong moment is the main culprit.

items.prepNote: a dry Savoyard wine or any dry white

content.text: A heavy saucepan or wide-based pot is essential; thin pans develop hot spots that cause the cheese to seize. If you have a ceramic fondue pot with a spirit burner, use that to keep the fondue warm at the table; otherwise a small hob-safe pot works fine.

Toss the grated {{comte}} of Comté and {{gruyere}} of Gruyère together in a bowl with the {{cornflour}} of cornflour until every strand is lightly coated. This step is what keeps the fondue smooth; do not skip it.

Rub the inside of the pot with the cut side of the {{garlic}} of garlic, pressing firmly so the oils are absorbed into the surface. Discard the garlic. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of dry white wine and warm it over medium heat until it just begins to simmer; do not let it boil hard.

Add the cheese mixture to the warm wine in three or four handfuls, stirring in a figure-of-eight with a wooden spoon after each addition. Wait until each batch has melted before adding the next. Keep the heat at medium; the cheese should melt smoothly into the wine without boiling. Once all the cheese is in and the mixture is smooth and glossy, stir in the {{kirsch}} of kirsch. Season with black pepper. Reduce the heat to the lowest setting and serve immediately.

Transfer the pot to a spirit burner or keep it on the lowest possible hob setting. Stir occasionally to prevent a skin forming on the surface. Each person impales a cube of {{baguette}} baguette on a long fork and stirs it into the cheese with a figure-of-eight movement, lifting out a good coating. As the fondue thickens near the end, a crust will form on the base of the pot. This is considered the best part: scrape it up with a fork and divide it between the table.

items.symptom: Fondue splits: oil on the surface with stringy cheese beneath
items.cause: Heat too high, or cheese added too quickly
items.fix: Remove from heat, add a splash of warm wine, and stir vigorously in figures-of-eight; return to low heat. Next time add the cheese in smaller batches and keep the heat at medium throughout

items.symptom: Fondue is lumpy rather than smooth
items.cause: Wine not warm enough when the cheese went in, or cheese too cold from the fridge
items.fix: Stir over low heat and add a small splash of warm wine; if lumps remain, pass through a sieve and return to the pot

items.symptom: Fondue thickens and becomes stiff as it sits
items.cause: Temperature has dropped; fondue firms up quickly when it cools
items.fix: Turn up the heat briefly and stir in a small splash of warm wine to loosen; keep the burner lit under the pot throughout the meal

items.symptom: Bread falls off the fork into the fondue
items.cause: Bread too soft, or fork lifted straight up rather than stirred
items.fix: Use bread that is one or two days old; stale bread clings better. Twist the fork in figures-of-eight to coat the bread before lifting

items.symptom: Fondue tastes flat or too mild
items.cause: Cheese not aged enough, or too little salt
items.fix: Use the most aged Comté and Gruyère you can find; 12-month Comté has far more flavour than a young one. Season with more black pepper and taste before serving

content.text: Fondue savoyarde comes from the high pasture country of the Savoie and Haute-Savoie, where the milk of mountain cattle produces the great Alpine cheeses. It is a practical dish: the cheeses keep through a long winter, the local wine is always to hand, and bread is the one thing available in quantity. The version from Switzerland is closely related but uses different cheeses, notably Vacherin Fribourgeois. The French version relies on Comté and Gruyère, and sometimes adds Emmental for a milder, more yielding texture. It is a dish of deep winter, eaten with friends around a single pot, and it does not improve by being hurried.
